tree.heading: 1. Introduction
tree.content_preview: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) is characterised by a rapid reduction in kidney function resulting in a failure to maintain fluid, electrolyte and acid-base homeostasis (Segaran et al, 2015). The term AKI e

tree.heading: 2. Identifying patients with AKI at risk of malnutrition
tree.content_preview: There is currently no validated screening tool to identify malnutrition in people with AKI. However, the Renal Nutrition Screening Tool, RNST (Xia et al, 2016) and the Renal Inpatient Nutrition Screen

tree.heading: 3. Principles of nutrition support in patients with AKI
tree.content_preview: The general aim of nutrition support is to maintain nutritional status whilst limiting the complications of AKI. This includes preventing or minimising PEW, preserving lean body mass, avoiding further

children.heading: 3.1 Dietetic classification of AKI
children.content_preview:
From a nutritional point of view patients can be divided into two groups:
 Patients with AKI in the non-catabolic state
 Patients with AKI in the catabolic state

ancestor_path: 3. Principles of nutrition support in patients with AKI

tree.heading: AKI in the non-catabolic state
tree.content_preview: Typically, patients with pre-renal or post-renal injuries present in a non-catabolic state. Common causes of AKI in a non-catabolic state include dehydration, certain medications and urinary obstructi

tree.heading: AKI in the catabolic state
tree.content_preview: Typically, patients with an intra-renal injury present in a catabolic state. Causes of AKI in a catabolic state include sepsis, acidosis and trauma. Patients often have multi-organ failure and are lik

children.heading: 3.2 The impact of Renal Replacement Therapy (RRT) on nutritional requirements
children.content_preview:
For patients receiving RRT as a treatment in AKI, nutritional requirements will be affected. All patients with AKI requiring RRT should be referred to the dietitian (Kanagasundaram et al, 2019).

tree.heading: Energy
tree.content_preview: AKI itself has no effect on the patient's energy requirements. Even in individuals with AKI and multiorgan failure, measured energy requirements are only 20%-30% above the estimated basal metabolic ra

tree.heading: Protein
tree.content_preview: Protein requirements are influenced by the patient's clinical condition, severity of AKI, presence or absence of a catabolic state and any RRT they may receive (Table 2). Recommendations for protein r

tree.heading: Electrolytes
tree.content_preview: AKI may be associated with significant electrolyte changes such as elevated potassium and phosphate levels. Electrolyte intake should be individualised according to the patient's blood biochemistry, c

tree.heading: Fluid
tree.content_preview: The medical team should advise on fluid provision taking into account clinical condition, stage of AKI and current treatment. Equations to estimate fluid requirements may be not useful in AKI. Instead

tree.heading: Micronutrients

tree.heading: 4. Monitoring
tree.content_preview: Where patients present with nutritional concerns it is important that they receive regular dietetic review throughout the course of treatment and during recovery. Energy, protein and fluid requirement

tree.heading: 6. Dietetic audit measures in AKI
